title: Understand: How Women Won the Vote
description: Check your understanding of the steps activists took to secure women the right to vote with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Understand: How Women Won the Vote

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Nineteenth Amendment, passed in 1920, banned voting discrimination on the basis of \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Unmarried women who owned land could vote in state and local elections in which state between 1776 and 1807?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**A gathering of women at the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848 produced this famous document, which listed  grievances and drew attention to inequalities and oppressive laws directed at women.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**In addition to a demand for the right to vote, what else was a demand that women made during the Seneca Falls Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The women’s suffrage amendment stated: “The right of citizens of the United States to vote shall not be denied or abridged by the United States or by any State on account of sex.” 

**Introduced in Congress as early as 1878, the amendment was named for which leader of the women’s suffrage movement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]]]

**What did the Supreme Court say in its decision in *Minor v. Happersett* (1875)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women are U.S. citizens, but voting is not a privilege of national citizenship.
Women are not U.S. citizens under the Fourteenth Amendment.
Women have the right to vote because they are citizens of the United States.
Women can vote despite the fact that they are not citizens.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the concept of “virtual representation”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Husbands represented their wives' interests in politics, making women’s right to vote unnecessary.
Women could serve as representatives in the House, but they could not cast a vote on laws.
Each man was assigned a woman and was required to conference with her before casting any vote.
Unmarried women could participate in the political process until they were married; then, their husbands would have two votes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which TWO suffragists organized the Seneca Falls Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What was the main purpose of the Seneca Falls Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To discuss and advocate for women’s rights
To advocate for the end of slavery
To propose a new suffrage amendment
To argue against the Fifteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**Often referred to as the Susan B. Anthony amendment, the Nineteenth Amendment aimed to extend suffrage to which group of people?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which state was the crucial last state that secured the ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of these statements about women’s right to vote before 1920 is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Some states allowed women to vote in state, local, or federal elections
No states allowed women to vote in all levels of elections, including in federal elections.
No states allowed women to vote in any elections, as there was no amendment giving women the right to vote.
Every state allowed women to vote in state and local elections, but women could not vote in federal elections.
